narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 41 compared to the usual 36.70, you brought some impressive strengths to your law school application. Your background also includes lower-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 68.8%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize candidates with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier relative to peers. This outweighed your LSAT score, which was a significant positive force, actively pulling the prediction toward success by demonstrating your strong test-taking ability. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average 5.71, added further concern, reinforcing a pattern of underperformance in school rankings compared to typical students. While your higher-than-average undergrad GPA and LSAT helped, they couldn't fully offset these lower deciles, which the model sees as the strongest indicators of bar exam struggles in similar profiles. For students like you, who start strong in undergrad but dip in later school deciles, the model is cautious because historical patterns show this often correlates with first-try bar failures more than test scores alone. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a lower-middle income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort—but the model weighs these academic deciles heavily for their predictive power. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent academic deciles through stronger performance in advanced courses or targeted tutoring, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. Consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, or seek programs that value your full-time commitment and GPA. With persistence, you can reapply stronger, turning this into a stepping stone rather than a setback.
